region_of_origin: Lucknow, Uttar Pradesh, India (served in Singapore)

ontology.foodex2_description: Rice-based mixed dish with meat and spices
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: No FoodEx2 code specific to Lucknowi Biryani; A0FHR (rice-based mixed dish) is the closest defensible category anchor.

allergens: Gluten risk from shared kitchen environments

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard restaurant plate of Lucknowi Biryani weighs approximately 500 g, providing around 750 kcal per serving.

nutrient_source.title: Nutritive Value of Indian Foods — Biryani (chicken, rice-based mixed dish)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: ICMR-NIN Nutritive Value of Indian Foods (2017 edition) provides the closest approved reference for chicken biryani as a composite rice-based dish. Per-100g values from the input record (provenance: ai_generated, confidence 0.7) are cross-referenced against ICMR-NIN chicken biryani composition data. Values are consistent with ICMR-NIN ranges for a dum-style chicken biryani (energy ~150 kcal/100g, protein ~7g, fat ~6g, carbohydrate ~17g). USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy used as secondary cross-check for plausibility. Monounsaturated fat, polyunsaturated fat, potassium, and water data not available in ICMR-NIN for this specific preparation; set to -1.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC; basmati rice, white, boiled (GI 58)
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Lucknowi Biryani as a composite dish. GI of 58 (medium) is estimated from the dominant carbohydrate component — basmati rice, white, boiled — as published in Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC (2008), International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values, Diabetes Care 31(12):2281-2283, indexed in the University of Sydney GI Database (https://glycemicindex.com/). Glycemic load per serving calculated as: GL = (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (58 × 85g) / 100 = 49.3. Carbohydrate per serving = 17g/100g × 500g = 85g.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 180 mg/100g; restaurant preparations may significantly exceed this; saturated fat 3g/100g from ghee adds cardiovascular load
hypertension.guidance: Limit serving to 300g or less to keep sodium intake below 540mg per meal; request reduced ghee and confirm salt levels with the restaurant, as commercial preparations commonly exceed 300mg sodium/100g.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Basmati rice GI 58 (medium); carbohydrate 17g/100g with only 1g/100g dietary fibre; a 500g serving delivers 85g carbohydrate and a glycemic load of approximately 49
type2_diabetes.guidance: Restrict portion to 150–200g to limit glycemic load to approximately 15–20 per meal; pair with plain yogurt (raita) to slow gastric emptying, and monitor postprandial blood glucose as restaurant biryani portions are typically large.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat 3g/100g from ghee; cholesterol 24mg/100g; a 500g serving provides 15g saturated fat, approaching the daily limit of 20g for CVD patients
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Choose a 200–250g portion to limit saturated fat to 6–7.5g per meal; request minimal ghee preparation and opt for chicken over lamb to reduce saturated fat and arachidonic acid intake.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Ghee contributes saturated fat that promotes pro-inflammatory cytokines (TNF-alpha, IL-6); if lamb is used, arachidonic acid (omega-6) amplifies inflammatory pathways; dietary fibre is low at 1g/100g
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Choose chicken biryani over lamb to reduce arachidonic acid load, request minimal ghee preparation, and note that anti-inflammatory spices (cinnamon, cloves, ginger) provide partial benefit but do not offset the inflammatory potential of ghee and red meat.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Lucknowi Biryani (also called Awadhi Biryani or Lakhnavi Biryani) is a fragrant, layered rice dish originating from the royal Nawabi kitchens of Lucknow, Uttar Pradesh, India. It is prepared using the dum cooking method, in which partially cooked basmati rice and marinated chicken (or lamb) are layered in a sealed pot and slow-steamed over low heat, allowing the aromatics to infuse the rice and meat simultaneously. The marinade typically includes yogurt, ginger, garlic, saffron, and whole spices such as cardamom, cloves, cinnamon, and bay leaf. Ghee (clarified butter) is used generously, contributing to the dish's characteristic richness and aroma. Rose water and kewra (screwpine) essence are hallmarks of the Lucknowi style, distinguishing it from the spicier Hyderabadi biryani. The texture is soft and moist, with each grain of rice remaining separate and perfumed with saffron. Per 100g, the dish provides approximately 150 kcal, 7g protein, 6g fat (3g saturated), 17g carbohydrate, 1g dietary fibre, and 180mg sodium. The glycemic index is estimated at 58 (medium) based on basmati rice as the dominant carbohydrate source per the University of Sydney GI Database. A standard restaurant serving of 500g delivers approximately 750 kcal and 85g carbohydrate, making portion control critical for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes or cardiovascular disease. The dish is halal-certified in most Singapore restaurant contexts. Clinically, the moderate sodium content, saturated fat from ghee, and high glycemic load per serving are the primary nutritional concerns for hypertension, Type 2 Diabetes, and cardiovascular disease management.
common_user_queries: How many calories in Lucknowi Biryani?
What is the difference between Lucknowi and Hyderabadi Biryani?
What spices are in Lucknowi Biryani?
How much sodium is in biryani?
Is Awadhi Biryani the same as Lucknowi Biryani?

confidence_reason: Nutrient values are consistent with ICMR-NIN (2017) chicken biryani composition data and cross-checked against USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy for plausibility. Original input provenance is ai_generated with confidence 0.7 from grabfood; values have been validated against approved sources and are plausible for a dum-style chicken biryani. GI is an analogous estimate based on basmati rice GI of 58 from the University of Sydney GI Database (Atkinson et al. 2008). Confidence is medium rather than high because no exact Lucknowi Biryani entry exists in any approved primary source; the record is adapted from the closest approved equivalents.
notes: monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, potassium_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are set to -1: detailed fatty acid profile, micronutrient panel, and moisture data for Lucknowi Biryani as a composite dum-cooked dish are not available in ICMR-NIN Nutritive Value of Indian Foods (2017) or USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy as of 2026. trans_fat_g is 0.1g/100g as reported in the input record; this is a trace level consistent with ghee-containing preparations. The dish description references both chicken and lamb in the health_suitability section of the input; the canonical record is based on chicken as the primary protein, consistent with the input description. The duplicate portion entry in the input (two identical '1 plate / 500g' records) has been deduplicated to a single serving entry. Country of origin in the input was listed as Singapore, which reflects the serving context; region of origin has been corrected to Lucknow, Uttar Pradesh, India. The ethnic_tags in the input included 'Naan' which is not relevant to biryani and has been removed from the output classification. Nutrient source URL updated to ICMR-NIN homepage (https://www.nin.res.in/) as the direct PDF link is a document download rather than a stable landing page; the ICMR-NIN Nutritive Value of Indian Foods (2017) is the authoritative approved source for Indian food composition data.
